subject: civic
learning_objectives: Define communal society and inter-communal relationship
Explain economic, social, and political activities in communal societies
Discuss values and skills that promote inter-communal relationships

sections.heading: Meaning and Characteristics of Communal Society
sections.content: A communal society is usually small and homogeneous. Most people are engaged in primary activities such as farming. Many people may have limited formal education, houses are often dispersed, and relationships are close, personal, and built on communal responsibility and solidarity.

sections.heading: Meaning of Inter-Communal Relationship
sections.content: Inter-communal relationship is the relationship between people from different communal societies. These relationships are built around economic, social, and political interactions between communities.

sections.heading: Economic, Social, and Political Activities
sections.content:
Economic activities include farming, harvesting, pot-making, mat weaving, blacksmithing, hunting, and traditional medicine.

Social activities include inter-community marriages, common worship, maintenance of markets, digging of wells and ponds, and clearing of roads.

Political activities include age-grade groupings, selection of leaders, enforcement of customs, punishment of offenders, and assignment of communal roles.

sections.heading: Values and Skills That Promote Inter-Communal Relationship
sections.content: Tolerance, cooperation, mutual respect, dialogue, justice, fairness, peaceful coexistence, and good governance help to strengthen inter-communal relationships.
